title: Big O — How Fast Does Code Scale? (Beginner)
key_insight: Big O tells you how code behaves when your data gets 10x bigger. This is the only metric that matters at scale.
body:
## The Common Big O Values

```python
# O(1): dictionary lookup
vocab = {'hello': 7592}
id = vocab['hello']   # instant always

# O(n): loop over n items
def find_max(arr):
    for x in arr: ...   # 10x bigger = 10x longer

# O(n²): nested loops
for i in arr:
    for j in arr:       # 10x bigger = 100x longer
        compare(i, j)
```

## What This Means at Scale

| n | O(1) | O(n) | O(n²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | instant | 0.0001s | 0.01s |
| 1,000,000 | instant | 1s | ~11 days |

Transformer attention is O(n²) in sequence length — this is why long contexts cost so much.
common_mistakes: Thinking O(n²) is fine for small data — it is, until data grows
Not recognising nested loops as O(n²)
Optimising before measuring — profile first, then optimise the bottleneck
mini_exercise: Look at the transformer attention mechanism. Every token attends to every other token. If a sequence has n tokens, how many comparisons? Write it as O(?). Why does this matter for long-context models?
